volumeTitle: Antigone. The Women of Trachis. Philoctetes. Oedipus at Colonus
label: Oedipus at Colonus (Section 654 - Section 821)

sections.label: Section 654
sections.original: ὅρα με λείπων
sections.translation: Beware that if you leave me —

sections.label: Section 654a
sections.original: μὴ δίδασχʼ ἃ χρή με δρᾶν .
sections.translation: Do not instruct me in my duties .

sections.label: Section 655
sections.original: ὀκνοῦντʼ ἀνάγκη .
sections.translation: Fear constrains me —

sections.label: Section 655a
sections.original: τοὐμὸν οὐκ ὀκνεῖ κέαρ .
sections.translation: My heart feels no fear .

sections.label: Section 656
sections.original: οὐκ οἶσθʼ ἀπειλὰς
sections.translation: You do not know the threats —

sections.label: Section 656a
sections.original: οἶδʼ ἐγώ σε μή τινα
sections.translation: I know that none will lead you from here against my will . Often threats have blustered in men ’ s hearts with words loud and vain ; but when the mind comes to itself once more ,

sections.label: Section 660
sections.original: αὑτοῦ γένηται , φροῦδα τἀπειλήματα .
sections.translation: the threats have vanished . For those men , too , perhaps — yes , even if in boldness they have spoken dreadful things of bringing you back , the voyage here will prove long and hard to sail . Now I exhort you , apart from any decision of mine , to take heart ,

sections.label: Section 665
sections.original: γνώμης ἐπαινῶ , Φοῖβος εἰ προὔπεμψέ σε ·
sections.translation: if indeed Phoebus has been your escort here . Even if I am not present , still my name , I know , will shield you from harm .

sections.label: Section 1.668
sections.original: εὐίππου , ξένε , τᾶσδε χώρας
sections.translation: Stranger , in this land of fine horses you have come to earth ’ s fairest home , the shining Colonus .

sections.label: Section 1.670
sections.original: τὸν ἀργῆτα Κολωνόν , ἔνθʼ
sections.translation: Here the nightingale , a constant guest , trills her clear note under the trees of green glades , dwelling amid the wine-dark ivy

sections.label: Section 1.675
sections.original: καὶ τὰν ἄβατον θεοῦ
sections.translation: and the god ’ s inviolate foliage , rich in berries and fruit , unvisited by sun , unvexed by the wind of any storm . Here the reveller Dionysus ever walks the ground ,

sections.label: Section 1.680
sections.original: θεαῖς ἀμφιπολῶν τιθήναις .
sections.translation: companion of the nymphs that nursed him .

sections.label: Section 1.681
sections.original: θάλλει δʼ οὐρανίας ὑπʼ ἄχνας
sections.translation: And , fed on heavenly dew , the narcissus blooms day by day with its fair clusters ; it is the ancient crown of the Great Goddesses .

sections.label: Section 1.685
sections.original: χρυσαυγὴς κρόκος · οὐδʼ ἄϋπνοι
sections.translation: And the crocus blooms with a golden gleam . Nor do the ever-flowing springs diminish , from which the waters of Cephisus wander , and each day with pure

sections.label: Section 1.690
sections.original: ἀκηράτῳ σὺν ὄμβρῳ
sections.translation: current it moves over the plains of the land ’ s swelling bosom , bringing fertility . Nor have the dancing Muses shunned this place , nor Aphrodite of the golden rein .

sections.label: Section 2.695
sections.original: ἔστιν δʼ οἷον ἐγὼ γᾶς Ἀσίας οὐκ ἐπακούω
sections.translation: nor as ever yet born in the great Dorian isle of Pelops : a plant unconquered , self-renewing , causing terror to destroying enemies .

sections.label: Section 2.700
sections.original: ὃ τᾷδε θάλλει μέγιστα χώρᾳ ,
sections.translation: It greatly flourishes in this land — the gray-leafed olive , nurturer of children . Youth cannot harm it by the ravages of his hand , nor can any who lives with old age . For the sleepless eye

sections.label: Section 2.705
sections.original: λεύσσει νιν μορίου Διὸς
sections.translation: of Zeus Morios watches over it , and gray-eyed Athena .

sections.label: Section 2.707
sections.original: ἄλλον δʼ αἶνον ἔχω ματροπόλει τᾷδε κράτιστον
sections.translation: And I have more praise for this city our mother ,

sections.label: Section 2.710
sections.original: δῶρον τοῦ μεγάλου δαίμονος , εἰπεῖν , χθονὸς αὔχημα μέγιστον ,
sections.translation:
the gift of a great divinity , a glory most great : the might of horses , the might of colts , and the might of the sea .
For you , son of Cronus , lord Poseidon , have set her on the throne of this pride ,

sections.label: Section 2.715
sections.original: ἵπποισιν τὸν ἀκεστῆρα χαλινὸν
sections.translation: by establishing first in our roads the bit that cures the rage of horses . And the shapely oar , well-fitted for the sea , in flying past the land leaps to follow the hundred-footed Nereids .

sections.label: Section 720
sections.original: ὦ πλεῖστʼ ἐπαίνοις εὐλογούμενον πέδον ,
sections.translation: Land that is praised above all lands , now it is your task to make those bright praises seen in deeds !

sections.label: Section 722
sections.original: τί δʼ ἔστιν , ὦ παῖ , καινόν ;
sections.translation: What strange new thing has happened , my daughter ?

sections.label: Section 722a
sections.original: ἆσσον ἔρχεται
sections.translation: Creon there draws near us , and not without followers , father .

sections.label: Section 724
sections.original: ὦ φίλτατοι γέροντες , ἐξ ὑμῶν ἐμοὶ
sections.translation: Ah , dearest old men , now give me

sections.label: Section 725
sections.original: φαίνοιτʼ ἂν ἤδη τέρμα τῆς σωτηρίας .
sections.translation: the final proof of my salvation !

sections.label: Section 726
sections.original: θάρσει , παρέσται · καὶ γὰρ εἰ γέρων ἐγώ ,
sections.translation: Courage ! It will be yours . For even if I am aged , this country ’ s strength has not grown old .

sections.label: Section 728
sections.original: ἄνδρες χθονὸς τῆσδʼ εὐγενεῖς οἰκήτορες ,
sections.translation: Gentlemen , noble dwellers in this land , I see from your eyes that a sudden fear has troubled you at my coming ;

sections.label: Section 730
sections.original: φόβον νεώρη τῆς ἐμῆς ἐπεισόδου ,
sections.translation:
but do not shrink back from me , and let no evil word escape you .
I am here with no thought of force ; I am old , and I know that the city to which I have come is mighty , if any in Hellas has might .

sections.label: Section 735
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἄνδρα τόνδε τηλικόσδʼ ἀπεστάλην
sections.translation: No , I have been sent , aged as I am , to plead with this man to return with me to the land of Cadmus . I am not one man ’ s envoy , but have a mandate from all our people ; since it belonged to me , by family , beyond all other Thebans to mourn his woes .

sections.label: Section 740
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ὦ ταλαίπωρʼ Οἰδίπους , κλύων ἐμοῦ
sections.translation: Unhappy Oedipus , hear us , and come home ! Justly are you summoned by all the Cadmeans , and most of all by me , since I — unless I am the worst of all men born — feel most sorrow for your woes , old man ,

sections.label: Section 745
sections.original: ὁρῶν σε τὸν δύστηνον ὄντα μὲν ξένον ,
sections.translation: when I see you , unhappy as you are , a stranger and a wanderer evermore , roaming in beggary , with one handmaid for your support . Ah , me , I had not thought that she could fall to such a depth of misery as that to which she has fallen —

sections.label: Section 750
sections.original: ἀεί σε κηδεύουσα καὶ τὸ σὸν κάρα
sections.translation:
this poor girl !— as she tends forever your dark life amid poverty ; in ripe youth , but unwed : a prize for the first passerby to seize .
Is it not a cruel reproach — alas !— that I have cast at you , and me , and all our race ?

sections.label: Section 755
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὐ γὰρ ἔστι τἀμφανῆ κρύπτειν , σύ νιν
sections.translation: But indeed an open shame cannot be hidden . Oedipus , in the name of your ancestral gods , listen to me ! Hide it , and consent to return to the city and the house of your ancestors , after bidding a kind farewell to this city . Athens is worthy ; yet your own city has the first claim on your reverence ,

sections.label: Section 760
sections.original: δίκῃ σέβοιτʼ ἄν , οὖσα σὴ πάλαι τροφός .
sections.translation: since it was Thebes that nurtured you long ago .

sections.label: Section 761
sections.original: ὦ πάντα τολμῶν κἀπὸ παντὸς ἂν φέρων
sections.translation: You who will dare anything , who from any just plea would derive a crafty trick , why do you make this attempt on me , and seek once more to snare me in your trap where I would feel most grief ?

sections.label: Section 765
sections.original: πρόσθεν τε γάρ με τοῖσιν οἰκείοις κακοῖς
sections.translation: Long ago , when I labored under the sickness of my self-made evils , and I yearned to be cast out of the land , you refused to grant the favor . But when my fierce anger had spent its force , and seclusion in the house was sweet to me ,

sections.label: Section 770
sections.original: τότʼ ἐξεώθεις κἀξέβαλλες , οὐδέ σοι
sections.translation: it was then that you thrust me from the house and cast me from the land . And this common race that you mention — that was not at all dear to you then . Now , in turn , when you see that I have a kindly welcome from this city and all its race , you try to pluck me away , wrapping your cruel thoughts in soft words .

sections.label: Section 775
sections.original: καίτοι τίς αὕτη τέρψις ἄκοντας φιλεῖν ;
sections.translation: And yet what pleasure do you find in this , in treating me as dear against my will ? As if a man should refuse you a gift , bring you no aid , when you continually begged for it ; but after your heart was sated with your desires , he should grant it then , when the favor could bring no joy

sections.label: Section 780
sections.original: ἆρʼ ἂν ματαίου τῆσδʼ ἂν ἡδονῆς τύχοις ;
sections.translation: — would you not find your delight in this empty ? Yet such is the nature of your own offers to me : noble in appearance , but in substance base . And I will declare it to these men too , to show you up as base . You have come to get me ,

sections.label: Section 785
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ὡς πάραυλον οἰκίσῃς , πόλις δέ σοι
sections.translation: not to bring me home , but to plant me near your borders , so that your city might escape uninjured by evils from this land . That fate is not for you , but this one : the brooding of my vengeful spirit on your land forever ; and for my sons , this heirloom :

sections.label: Section 790
sections.original: χθονὸς λαχεῖν τοσοῦτον , ἐνθανεῖν μόνον .
sections.translation:
just so much soil in my realm in which to die .
Am I not wiser than you in the fortunes of Thebes ? Yes , far wiser , by as much as the sources of my knowledge are truer : Phoebus I mean , and his father , Zeus himself . But you have come here with fraud on your lips , yes ,

sections.label: Section 795
sections.original: πολλὴν ἔχον στόμωσιν · ἐν δὲ τῷ λέγειν
sections.translation: and with a tongue keener than the edge of a sword ; yet by their use you may well reap more sorrow than salvation . Still , since I know that I cannot persuade you of this , go ! Allow us to live on here ; for even in this plight our life would not be bad , if we should be content with it .

sections.label: Section 800
sections.original: πότερα νομίζεις δυστυχεῖν ἔμʼ ἐς τὰ σά ,
sections.translation: Which of us , do you think , suffers more in this exchange — I by your action , or you by your own ?

sections.label: Section 802
sections.original: ἐμοὶ μέν ἐσθʼ ἥδιστον , εἰ σὺ μήτʼ ἐμὲ
sections.translation: For me , it is enough if your pleading fails both with me and with these men nearby .

sections.label: Section 804
sections.original: ὦ δύσμορʼ , οὐδὲ τῷ χρόνῳ φύσας φανεῖ
sections.translation: Unhappy man , will you let everyone see that even in your years you have gained no sense ?

sections.label: Section 805
sections.original: φρένας ποτʼ ἀλλὰ λῦμα τῷ γήρᾳ τρέφει ;
sections.translation: Must you live on to disgrace your old age ?

sections.label: Section 806
sections.original: γλώσσῃ σὺ δεινός · ἄνδρα δʼ οὐδένʼ οἶδʼ ἐγὼ
sections.translation: You have a clever tongue , but I know no just man who can produce from every side a pretty speech .

sections.label: Section 808
sections.original: χωρὶς τό τʼ εἰπεῖν πολλὰ καὶ τὰ καίρια .
sections.translation: Words may be many , and yet not to the point .

sections.label: Section 809
sections.original: ὡς δὴ σὺ βραχέα , ταῦτα δʼ ἐν καιρῷ λέγεις .
sections.translation: As if yours , indeed , were few , but on the mark .

sections.label: Section 810
sections.original: οὐ δῆθʼ ὅτῳ γε νοῦς ἴσος καὶ σοὶ πάρα .
sections.translation: They cannot be , not for one whose mind is such as yours .

sections.label: Section 811
sections.original: ἄπελθʼ , ἐρῶ γὰρ καὶ πρὸ τῶνδε , μηδέ με
sections.translation: Begone ! I will say it for these men too . And do not besiege me with a jealous watch where I am destined to remain .

sections.label: Section 813
sections.original: μαρτύρομαι τούσδʼ , οὐ σέ · πρὸς δὲ τοὺς φίλους
sections.translation: I call these men , and not you , to witness the tenor of your words to your friends . And if I ever catch you —

sections.label: Section 815
sections.original: τίς δʼ ἄν με τῶνδε συμμάχων ἕλοι βίᾳ ;
sections.translation: And who could catch me against the will of these allies ?

sections.label: Section 816
sections.original: ἦ μὴν σὺ κἄνευ τοῦδε λυπηθεὶς ἔσει .
sections.translation: I promise you , soon you will be pained even without that .

sections.label: Section 817
sections.original: ποίῳ σὺν ἔργῳ τοῦτʼ ἀπειλήσας ἔχεις ;
sections.translation: Where is the deed which backs that threatening word ?

sections.label: Section 818
sections.original: παίδοιν δυοῖν σοι τὴν μὲν ἀρτίως ἐγὼ
sections.translation: One of your two daughters I have myself just seized and sent away . The other I will drag off immediately .

sections.label: Section 820
sections.original: οἴμοι .
sections.translation: Oh , no !

sections.label: Section 820a
sections.original: τάχʼ ἕξεις μᾶλλον οἰμώζειν τάδε .
sections.translation: You will soon find more to weep about .

sections.label: Section 821
sections.original: τὴν παῖδʼ ἔχεις μου ;
sections.translation: You have my child ?
